startScreenOptions.title: PY4.1-PY4.9 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for PY4.1-PY4.9.

interactions.label: Gastrin stimulates acid secretion from parietal cells. Which hormone provides th

params.question: Gastrin stimulates acid secretion from parietal cells. Which hormone provides the negative feedback that switches gastrin off when gastric acidity rises?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Secretin is released by S cells in the duodenum in response to acid entering from the stomach. It stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion to neutralise that acid, but it does not directly inhibit gastrin release from G cells.
CCK is released by I cells in response to fat and protein in the duodenum. It triggers pancreatic enzyme secretion and gallbladder contraction but does not form a feedback loop with gastrin.

Correct. When gastric pH drops below 2, D cells in the antral mucosa release somatostatin, which directly inhibits gastrin release from neighbouring G cells. This is a classic paracrine negative feedback loop.

GIP is released by K cells in the duodenum in response to fat and carbohydrate. It suppresses gastric acid secretion and stimulates insulin release, but it does not directly inhibit gastrin at the G-cell level.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the gastrin–somatostatin feedback loop. When gastric pH falls below 2, D cells release somatostatin, which acts locally on G cells to shut down gastrin secretion.
Well done. The gastrin–somatostatin axis is a textbook example of paracrine negative feedback in the gut. Rising acidity activates D cells, which suppress G cells locally.

interactions.label: Which phase of gastric acid secretion contributes the largest share of total aci

params.question: Which phase of gastric acid secretion contributes the largest share of total acid output, and what is its primary stimulus?

answers.text: Cephalic phase — vagal stimulation from sight and smell of food

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The cephalic phase does involve vagal stimulation triggered by the sight or thought of food, but it accounts for only about 30% of total acid output, not the largest share.

answers.text: Gastric phase — gastrin release triggered by stomach distension

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The gastric phase contributes approximately 60% of total acid output. When food distends the stomach wall, G cells release gastrin, which is the dominant driver of acid secretion.

answers.text: Intestinal phase — secretin release when chyme enters the duodenum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The intestinal phase contributes only about 10% and is primarily inhibitory. Secretin released here actually suppresses further acid secretion rather than stimulating it.

answers.text: Gastric phase — parasympathetic stimulation from Meissner's plexus

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While the gastric phase is indeed the largest contributor, its primary stimulus is gastrin from G cells, not Meissner's plexus. Meissner's plexus regulates mucosal secretion and blood flow but is not the main driver of acid output in this phase.

overallFeedback.feedback: The three phases contribute roughly 30% (cephalic), 60% (gastric), and 10% (intestinal). The gastric phase is dominant because food in the stomach directly stimulates G cells to release gastrin.
Excellent. The gastric phase dominates acid secretion (60%), driven by gastrin from antral G cells. Remember the 30-60-10 rule for cephalic, gastric, and intestinal phases.

interactions.label: Under chronic stress, sympathetic stimulation suppresses Brunner's gland secreti

params.question: Under chronic stress, sympathetic stimulation suppresses Brunner's gland secretion, reducing the alkaline mucus barrier in the duodenum and increasing the risk of duodenal ulcers.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This statement is true. Brunner's glands are activated by parasympathetic (vagal) input and suppressed by sympathetic stimulation. During chronic stress, sympathetic tone dominates, reducing the protective alkaline mucus layer in the duodenum and predisposing to ulcer formation.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Brunner's glands in the duodenal submucosa secrete alkaline mucus that shields the duodenal wall from gastric acid. Vagal stimulation activates them, but sympathetic dominance during stress suppresses them, leaving the mucosa vulnerable to acid injury.

interactions.label: During fasting, the small intestine undergoes cyclical contractions every 90 min

params.question: During fasting, the small intestine undergoes cyclical contractions every 90 minutes to sweep residual contents forward. Which hormone drives this migrating myoelectric complex?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Gastrin stimulates gastric acid secretion and motility in the fed state. It does not drive the fasting housekeeping contractions of the migrating myoelectric complex.
CCK is released postprandially in response to fat and protein. It actually interrupts the MMC pattern when feeding begins, rather than driving it.

Correct. Motilin is released cyclically during fasting and drives the migrating myoelectric complex. Erythromycin, a motilin receptor agonist, is used clinically as a prokinetic drug precisely because of this mechanism.
Secretin is released when acid enters the duodenum and drives pancreatic bicarbonate secretion. It plays no role in the cyclical fasting contractions of the MMC.

overallFeedback.feedback: Motilin is the hormone that drives the migrating myoelectric complex every 90 minutes during fasting. A useful clinical link: erythromycin acts as a motilin agonist and is used as a prokinetic.
Well done. Motilin drives the MMC during fasting. When these contractions are disrupted, bacteria accumulate in the small intestine, causing small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO).

interactions.label: The majority of the body's serotonin is produced in the brain, where it regulate

params.question: The majority of the body's serotonin is produced in the brain, where it regulates mood and behaviour.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This statement is false. While serotonin does regulate mood in the brain, 95% of the body's total serotonin is actually produced in the gut by enterochromaffin cells. It regulates gut motility and signals satiety to the brain. This gut-brain serotonin connection explains why SSRIs frequently cause GI side effects like nausea and diarrhoea.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. Approximately 95% of the body's serotonin is produced in the gut by enterochromaffin cells, not in the brain. Gut serotonin regulates motility and sends satiety signals to the brain via the vagus nerve. This is why SSRIs commonly cause gastrointestinal side effects.

interactions.label: During abdominal examination, you suspect splenomegaly. From which region should

params.question: During abdominal examination, you suspect splenomegaly. From which region should you begin palpation to avoid missing a grossly enlarged spleen?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Starting at the left hypochondrium risks pressing directly onto a massively enlarged spleen without detecting its lower edge. You may miss the extent of enlargement entirely.

The epigastrium is the starting point for liver palpation. The spleen enlarges diagonally toward the right iliac fossa, so a lateral approach from the epigastrium would not follow the correct axis of splenic enlargement.

answers.text: Right iliac fossa, sweeping toward the left hypochondrium

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The spleen enlarges downward and to the right. Starting from the right iliac fossa and sweeping diagonally upward toward the left hypochondrium on inspiration ensures you detect even a massively enlarged spleen.
A non-directed approach from the umbilicus lacks the systematic diagonal sweep needed to follow the spleen's axis of enlargement. You risk missing the splenic edge altogether.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: the spleen enlarges diagonally toward the right iliac fossa. Begin palpation there and sweep upward toward the left hypochondrium, asking the patient to breathe deeply so the diaphragm pushes the spleen into your examining hand.

Well done. Starting from the right iliac fossa and sweeping toward the left hypochondrium on inspiration is the correct technique. The spleen must enlarge to three times its normal size before it becomes palpable.
